title: Spring stimulative feeding for bees
subtitle: Encouraging early colony buildup before the nectar flow.
excerpt: Bees emerge from winter with depleted stores and a small cluster. In early spring, a small syrup feed can trigger the queen to start laying faster, building up the population in time for the first major nectar flow. Here's when to feed, what to use, and how much.
sourceNotes: NBU Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024), feeding guidance. BBKA Bee Craft seasonal notes. Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Internet Archive, spring management chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: A sugar solution fed to bees in spring (thin, 1:1 sugar to water) to stimulate laying, or in autumn (thick, 2:1) to top up winter stores.
Eggs, larvae, and pupae in the comb, the developing bees at all stages before they emerge as adults.
The tight ball of bees that forms in winter, generating heat to keep the colony alive through cold weather.

content.text: In late February and March, a
may still be holding tight and the queen may be laying only a small patch of

feed at this point mimics an early nectar flow and encourages the queen to expand the brood nest faster. The goal is to have the colony strong and populous by the time dandelions and fruit-tree blossom open in April.

Start when daytime temperatures stay reliably above 8°C and there's little or no natural forage yet. January and February are too early in most years. The bees can't move syrup far enough in deep cold, and you risk chilling the cluster. Late February to mid-March works in southern England. Mid-March to early April in Scotland.

Use a 1:1 syrup, roughly one part white sugar to one part warm water by weight or volume. This thin ratio is important: a thick 2:1 syrup is the autumn feed for building stores; a thin 1:1 syrup is the spring stimulative feed that the bees process and move as if it were nectar. Inverted sugar syrup sold in drums is a ready-made alternative and slightly easier for bees to process.

Give small amounts frequently rather than a large amount all at once. 500 ml every four to five days is a common approach. The aim is stimulation, not storage, a large dump of syrup fills the brood box with stored syrup and the queen runs out of cells to lay in. Watch the cluster's growth rate and stop feeding once there is a natural nectar flow in the area.
Contact feeder: a sealed tin or bucket with small holes in the lid, inverted over the crown board hole. Gravity drips syrup through the holes. Simple and cheap; refilled as needed.
Rapid feeder: a tray feeder that sits over the crown board hole and holds a litre or more. Bees access a central well from below. Easier to check without disturbing the hive.
Frame feeder: a narrow trough the width of a brood frame, inserted in place of a frame. Good for small, tight clusters that can't easily travel to a top feeder in cold weather.

attrs.title: Stop feeding before supers go on

content.text: Stop spring feeding at least two weeks before adding honey supers. Bees given syrup close to the honey season can store it in the super, which looks like honey but is not, the presence of inverted sugar confuses the flavour profile and the Weights and Measures rules for honey labelling. Wait for a clear gap between the last feed and the first day the supers go on.
